title: Find median of an array
description: Find the median value of an array using sorting

examples.explanation: Step 1: Sort array: [1, 2, 3]. Step 2: n = 3 (odd). Step 3: median = arr[3//2] = arr[1] = 2.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force via Sorting
approaches.summary: Sort the array in ascending order, then compute the median based on the sorted order. For an odd-length array, the median is the middle element; for even-length, it's the average of the two middle elements.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on sorting algorithm

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Median via Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete solution: sort array and compute median based on length parity
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of finding the median using the sorting approach. The intuition is that sorting brings order to the data, allowing us to easily identify the middle element(s). After sorting in ascending order, we check if the array length is odd or even. For odd length (n), the median is the element at index n//2 (0-based indexing). For even length, it's the average of elements at indices n//2 - 1 and n//2. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Sort the array using any O(n log n) sorting algorithm. 2) Calculate n = len(arr). 3) If n % 2 == 1: return arr[n//2]. 4) Else: return (arr[n//2 - 1] + arr[n//2]) / 2. Time complexity is O(n log n) dominated by sorting. Space complexity is O(1) for in-place sorting or O(n) for algorithms requiring extra space. This connects to L1 by using the sorted array to compute the median. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle even-length arrays correctly (need average of two middle elements), using integer division incorrectly for the average (should use float division), and not considering empty arrays. Real code pattern: def find_median(arr): arr.sort(); n = len(arr); if n % 2 == 1: return arr[n//2]; else: return (arr[n//2 - 1] + arr[n//2]) / 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort array: [1, 2, 3]. Step 2: n = 3 (odd). Step 3: median = arr[3//2] = arr[1] = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Median from Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array, calculate median based on length parity
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the median computation logic after sorting is complete. The intuition is that in a sorted array, the median's position depends solely on whether the length is odd or even. For odd length, there's exactly one middle element. For even length, there are two middle elements, and the median is their arithmetic mean. Step-by-step: 1) Input: sorted array arr. 2) n = len(arr). 3) If n == 0: handle error (return None or raise exception). 4) If n % 2 == 1: return arr[n//2]. 5) Else: return (arr[n//2 - 1] + arr[n//2]) / 2.0. Time complexity is O(1) since we're just accessing elements by index. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the final step after sorting. Common mistakes: using integer division for the average (should ensure float result), not handling empty arrays, and incorrect index calculation for even case. Real code pattern: def median_from_sorted(arr): n = len(arr); if n == 0: return None; if n % 2: return arr[n//2]; else: return (arr[n//2 - 1] + arr[n//2]) / 2.0.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 5 (odd). Step 2: median = arr[5//2] = arr[2] = 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sorting the Array
ladders.desc: Sort an array in ascending order using an efficient algorithm
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the sorting operation, which is the core of the approach. The intuition is that sorting organizes elements in non-decreasing order, making median identification trivial. We need an O(n log n) sorting algorithm for efficiency. Step-by-step using merge sort: 1) If array has 0 or 1 elements, return it (base case). 2) Find mid = len(arr)//2. 3) Recursively sort left half = arr[:mid]. 4) Recursively sort right half = arr[mid:]. 5) Merge sorted left and right halves. Time complexity is O(n log n). Space complexity is O(n) for merge sort due to auxiliary arrays. This connects to L1 by providing the sorted input. Common mistakes: using O(n²) sorts like bubble sort for large arrays, not handling edge cases (empty or single-element arrays), and incorrect implementation of merge step. Real code pattern: def merge_sort(arr): if len(arr) <= 1: return arr; mid = len(arr)//2; left = merge_sort(arr[:mid]); right = merge_sort(arr[mid:]); return merge(left, right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Split [3,1,2] into [3] and [1,2]. Step 2: [3] is sorted. Step 3: Sort [1,2]: split into [1] and [2], both sorted, merge to [1,2]. Step 4: Merge [3] and [1,2]: compare 3 and 1 → add 1; compare 3 and 2 → add 2; add remaining 3 → [1,2,3].

Step 1: Split [5,2,4,1,3] into [5,2] and [4,1,3]. Step 2: Sort [5,2]: split into [5] and [2], merge to [2,5]. Step 3: Sort [4,1,3]: split into [4] and [1,3]; sort [1,3] to [1,3]; merge [4] and [1,3] to [1,3,4]. Step 4: Merge [2,5] and [1,3,4]: compare 2 and 1 → add 1; compare 2 and 3 → add 2; compare 5 and 3 → add 3; compare 5 and 4 → add 4; add 5 → [1,2,3,4,5].

ladders.title: Building Block — Merge Two Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Combine two sorted arrays into one sorted array
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for merge sort. The intuition is that by comparing the smallest elements of each sorted array, we can build the combined sorted array incrementally. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize pointers i=0 for arr1, j=0 for arr2, and result array. 2) While i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): compare arr1[i] and arr2[j]. If arr1[i] <= arr2[j], append arr1[i] to result and i++. Else, append arr2[j] to result and j++. 3) After loop, append remaining elements from arr1 (if any) and arr2 (if any). Time complexity is O(n+m) where n,m are lengths. Space complexity is O(n+m) for the result array. This connects to L2 as the merge step in merge sort. Common mistakes: not handling empty arrays, incorrect comparison (should use <= for stability), and forgetting to append remaining elements. Real code pattern: def merge(arr1, arr2): i,j=0,0; result=[]; while i<len(arr1) and j<len(arr2): if arr1[i]<=arr2[j]: result.append(arr1[i]); i+=1; else: result.append(arr2[j]); j+=1; result.extend(arr1[i:]); result.extend(arr2[j:]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0,j=0: compare 1 and 2 → add 1, i=1. Step 2: i=1,j=0: compare 3 and 2 → add 2, j=1. Step 3: i=1,j=1: compare 3 and 4 → add 3, i=2. Step 4: arr1 done, add remaining [4] from arr2 → [1,2,3,4].

Step 1: i=0,j=0: compare 5 and 3 → add 3, j=1. Step 2: i=0,j=1: compare 5 and 7 → add 5, i=1. Step 3: i=1,j=1: compare 10 and 7 → add 7, j=2. Step 4: i=1,j=2: compare 10 and 12 → add 10, i=2. Step 5: arr1 done, add remaining [12] from arr2 → [3,5,7,10,12].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Append Elements
ladders.desc: Compare two numbers and append the smaller to a result array
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the building block of merging. The intuition is that by repeatedly comparing the current smallest elements from two sources, we can construct a sorted sequence. Step-by-step: 1) Have two numbers a and b, and a result list. 2) Compare a and b. 3) If a <= b, append a to result; otherwise append b. 4) Move to next elements in the respective sources. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1) for the operation itself. This connects to L3 as the core step in the merge loop. Common mistakes: using < instead of <= (can affect stability), not updating pointers correctly, and not handling cases where one source is exhausted. Real code pattern: if a <= b: result.append(a); else: result.append(b).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 3 and 5. Step 2: Since 3 <= 5, append 3 to result. Step 3: Result becomes [3].
Step 1: Compare 7 and 2. Step 2: Since 7 > 2, append 2 to result. Step 3: Result becomes [1,2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Length
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and determine array size
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to work with arrays. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices starting from 0. To find the median, we need to know the array length and access specific positions. Step-by-step: 1) For an array arr, len(arr) gives the number of elements. 2) Access element at index i using arr[i]. 3) For median calculation, we use indices like n//2 for odd length. Time complexity is O(1) for indexing and length. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 by providing the data structure for operations. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing (should be 0-based), accessing out-of-bounds indices, and not understanding integer division. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); middle_element = arr[n//2].

examples.trace: Step 1: len(arr) = 3. Step 2: arr[1] accesses element at index 1 (second element). Step 3: Value is 20.

examples.input: arr=[5,15,25,35], get length and middle index
examples.trace: Step 1: len(arr) = 4. Step 2: For even length, middle indices are n//2 - 1 and n//2. Step 3: n//2 = 4//2 = 2. Step 4: So middle_index = 2 (for second middle element).

approaches.name: Approach 2: QuickSelect Algorithm
approaches.summary: Use the QuickSelect algorithm to find the k-th smallest element without fully sorting the array. For median, find the middle element(s) based on array length, leveraging partitioning similar to quicksort.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) for in-place, O(log n) for recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Median Using QuickSelect
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of median finding using the QuickSelect algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The median is the middle value of a sorted array. For an array of length n, if n is odd, the median is the element at index n//2 (0-based). If n is even, the median is the average of the two middle elements at indices n//2 - 1 and n//2. QuickSelect efficiently finds the k-th smallest element without fully sorting the array, making it ideal for median finding with O(n) average time complexity. The intuition is that by recursively partitioning the array around a pivot, we can discard portions that cannot contain our target k-th element, similar to binary search but on unsorted data. For median, we need to find either one or two specific k values. The algorithm works by: 1) Choosing a pivot (commonly the last element). 2) Partitioning the array so elements ≤ pivot are on the left, > pivot on the right, returning the pivot's final index. 3) If the pivot index equals k, we've found our element. If k < pivot index, recurse on the left subarray; otherwise, recurse on the right. For even-length arrays, we find both middle elements and average them. Time complexity is O(n) average due to halving the search space on average, but O(n²) worst-case if pivots are poorly chosen (e.g., already sorted array). Space is O(1) for in-place partitioning, but O(log n) for recursion stack depth. Connection to L1: This uses the partition subroutine to divide the array. Common mistakes include: not handling duplicate values correctly in partition, forgetting base cases in recursion, and incorrect index calculations for median in even-length arrays. To avoid, ensure partition moves elements ≤ pivot left, test with arrays containing duplicates, and carefully compute k values for median.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array length n=6 (even), need k1 = n//2 - 1 = 2 (3rd smallest) and k2 = n//2 = 3 (4th smallest). Step 2: Find k1=2. Initial array [3,1,4,1,5,9], pivot=9, partition: compare elements ≤9: all are ≤9, so pivot index=5. Since 2<5, recurse left on [3,1,4,1,5]. New pivot=5, partition: elements ≤5: [3,1,4,1,5] → pivot index=4. Since 2<4, recurse left on [3,1,4,1]. New pivot=1, partition: elements ≤1: [1,1] move to left, others [3,4] right → array becomes [1,1,4,3], pivot index=1. Since 2>1, recurse right on [4,3] (from indices 2-3). New pivot=3, partition: elements ≤3: [3] → array [3,4], pivot index=2. Since 2==2, found k1 element=3. Step 3: Find k2=3. Similar process: start with original array, find 4th smallest. QuickSelect for k=3: after partitions, find element=4. Step 4: Median = (3+4)/2 = 3.5. Wait, correction: output should be 3.5, but let's recompute. Actually, sorted array is [1,1,3,4,5,9], median = (3+4)/2=3.5. In trace, k1=2 gives 3rd smallest=3, k2=3 gives 4th smallest=4, average=3.5. My output said 3.0, that's an error. Correct output: 3.5. Let's adjust trace: After finding k1=2 element=3 and k2=3 element=4, median = (3+4)/2 = 3.5.

Step 1: n=3 (odd), need k = n//2 = 1 (2nd smallest, 0-based). Step 2: QuickSelect for k=1 on [7,2,9]. Pivot=9, partition: elements ≤9: [7,2,9] → pivot index=2. Since 1<2, recurse left on [7,2]. New pivot=2, partition: elements ≤2: [2] → array [2,7], pivot index=0. Since 1>0, recurse right on [7] (index 1). Single element, return 7. Step 3: Found k=1 element=7, which is median. Sorted array is [2,7,9], median=7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Partition Function for QuickSelect
ladders.desc: Implement the partition function that rearranges elements around a pivot.
ladders.explanation: The partition function is the core of QuickSelect, responsible for dividing the array into two parts: elements less than or equal to the pivot on the left, and elements greater than the pivot on the right. It returns the final index of the pivot after rearrangement. The intuition is to select a pivot (often the last element), then iterate through the array, swapping elements to ensure all values ≤ pivot are moved to the left side. We maintain an index 'i' that tracks the boundary where the next smaller element should go. Start with i at the beginning (e.g., i = left). For each element from left to right-1, if it is ≤ pivot, swap it with the element at index i and increment i. Finally, swap the pivot (at the right index) with the element at i, placing the pivot in its correct sorted position. This ensures that all elements to the left of the pivot are ≤ pivot, and all to the right are > pivot. Time complexity is O(n) for a subarray of length n, as it requires a single pass. Space complexity is O(1) as it operates in-place. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively in QuickSelect to narrow down the search for the k-th element. Common mistakes include: incorrect comparison (using < instead of ≤ can mishandle duplicates), off-by-one errors in indices, and not swapping the pivot at the end. To avoid, test with arrays containing duplicates and edge cases like already sorted arrays. Real code pattern: function partition(arr, left, right): pivot = arr[right]; i = left; for j in range(left, right): if arr[j] <= pivot: swap arr[i] and arr[j]; i += 1; swap arr[i] and arr[right]; return i.

examples.trace: Step 1: pivot = arr[4] = 5. i = left = 0. Step 2: Iterate j from 0 to 3. j=0: arr[0]=3 ≤5, swap arr[0] with arr[0] (no change), i=1. j=1: arr[1]=1 ≤5, swap arr[1] with arr[1], i=2. j=2: arr[2]=4 ≤5, swap arr[2] with arr[2], i=3. j=3: arr[3]=1 ≤5, swap arr[3] with arr[3], i=4. Step 3: Swap pivot arr[4] with arr[i=4] (no change). Array remains [3,1,4,1,5], pivot index=4. All elements ≤5, so pivot stays at end.

Step 1: pivot = arr[4] = 8. i = 0. Step 2: j=0: arr[0]=9 >8, do nothing. j=1: arr[1]=2 ≤8, swap arr[1] with arr[i=0]: array becomes [2,9,6,3,8], i=1. j=2: arr[2]=6 ≤8, swap arr[2] with arr[i=1]: array becomes [2,6,9,3,8], i=2. j=3: arr[3]=3 ≤8, swap arr[3] with arr[i=2]: array becomes [2,6,3,9,8], i=3. Step 3: Swap pivot arr[4]=8 with arr[i=3]=9: array becomes [2,6,3,8,9]. Pivot index=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Selection of k-th Smallest Element
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive QuickSelect logic to find the k-th smallest element.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the recursive algorithm that uses partition to find the k-th smallest element in an unsorted array. The intuition is similar to binary search: after partitioning, the pivot's index tells us how many elements are less than or equal to it. If the pivot index equals k, we've found our element. If k is less than the pivot index, the k-th smallest must be in the left subarray; if greater, it must be in the right subarray. This reduces the problem size by approximately half on average, leading to O(n) average time. The algorithm: 1) If left == right, return the single element (base case). 2) Choose a pivot index (e.g., right). 3) Call partition to get the pivot's correct index p. 4) If k == p, return arr[p]. 5) If k < p, recursively call QuickSelect on the left subarray (left to p-1). 6) If k > p, recursively call on the right subarray (p+1 to right), adjusting k by subtracting p+1 since the right subarray starts after p. Time complexity: O(n) average as each recursion reduces n by about half, but O(n²) worst-case if pivots are bad. Space: O(log n) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the partition function to divide the array. Common mistakes: incorrect base case handling, forgetting to adjust k for right recursion, and infinite recursion if indices are wrong. To avoid, test with small arrays and ensure k is 0-based. Real code pattern: function quickSelect(arr, left, right, k): if left == right: return arr[left]; pivotIndex = partition(arr, left, right); if k == pivotIndex: return arr[pivotIndex]; elif k < pivotIndex: return quickSelect(arr, left, pivotIndex-1, k); else: return quickSelect(arr, pivotIndex+1, right, k).

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, k=2. Partition with pivot=5: as in L1 example, pivot index p=4. Since k=2 < p=4, recurse left on [3,1,4,1] (indices 0-3). Step 2: left=0, right=3, k=2. Partition with pivot=1: pivot index p=1. Since k=2 > p=1, recurse right on [4,1] (indices 2-3), adjust k = k - p - 1 = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0. Step 3: left=2, right=3, k=0. Partition with pivot=1: pivot index p=3. Since k=0 < p=3, recurse left on [4] (index 2). Step 4: left=2, right=2, k=0, base case return arr[2]=4. Wait, that gives 4, but expected 3. Let's correct: Original array [3,1,4,1,5], sorted is [1,1,3,4,5], k=2 should be 3. Error in trace: In step 2, after partition with pivot=1, array becomes [1,1,4,3] with p=1. Right subarray is indices 2-3: [4,3], k adjusted to 0. Then partition on [4,3] with pivot=3 gives p=3, but since k=0 < p=3, recurse left on [4] gives 4. This is wrong because k=0 in subarray [4,3] means smallest element, which is 3 after partition? Let's redo partition on [4,3]: pivot=3, i=2, j=2: arr[2]=4 >3, do nothing. Swap pivot arr[3]=3 with arr[i=2]=4: array becomes [3,4], p=2. Then k=0 < p=2, recurse left on [3] gives 3. So output 3. Correct trace: Step 3: left=2, right=3, k=0. Partition on [4,3]: pivot=3, i=2, j=2: no swap, swap pivot: array [3,4], p=2. Since k=0 < p=2, recurse left on [3] (index 2), base case return 3.

Step 1: left=0, right=2, k=1. Partition with pivot=9: p=2. Since k=1 < p=2, recurse left on [7,2] (indices 0-1). Step 2: left=0, right=1, k=1. Partition with pivot=2: p=0. Since k=1 > p=0, recurse right on [7] (index 1), adjust k = k - p - 1 = 1 - 0 - 1 = 0. Step 3: left=1, right=1, k=0, base case return arr[1]=7.

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Pivot Selection and Its Impact
ladders.desc: Learn how to choose a pivot and analyze its effect on algorithm performance.
ladders.explanation: Pivot selection is crucial in QuickSelect as it determines the efficiency of the partition step. The goal is to choose a pivot that splits the array into roughly equal halves, ensuring average O(n) time. Poor pivot choices (e.g., always picking the smallest or largest element) can lead to O(n²) worst-case time. Common strategies include: 1) Always pick the last element (simple but vulnerable to sorted arrays). 2) Pick a random element (reduces chance of worst-case). 3) Use median-of-three (pick median of first, middle, last elements) to better approximate the true median. The intuition is that a good pivot balances the left and right subarrays, minimizing recursive depth. For example, if the pivot is close to the median, each recursion reduces the problem size by about half. In contrast, a bad pivot only eliminates one element per step. Time analysis: With random pivot, expected time is O(n) due to probabilistic balancing. Space is unaffected. Connection to L2: The pivot is used in the partition function within QuickSelect. Common mistakes: not randomizing pivot in practice, leading to worst-case on sorted inputs. To avoid, implement random pivot selection or median-of-three. Real code pattern: For random pivot, swap a random index with the last element before partitioning. Example: index = random.randint(left, right); swap arr[index] and arr[right]; then proceed with pivot = arr[right]. This ensures all permutations are equally likely, making worst-case rare.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], using last element as pivot
examples.trace: Step 1: Always pick last element: pivot=5. Partition: all elements ≤5, so pivot stays at end, index=4. Left subarray is [1,2,3,4], right is empty. This is unbalanced because right subarray has 0 elements, so recursion only reduces size by 1 each step, leading to O(n²) time if array is sorted.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], using random pivot (e.g., index 2, value 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Randomly select index 2, value=3. Swap with last element: array becomes [1,2,5,4,3]. Partition with pivot=3: elements ≤3: [1,2,3] move left, others [5,4] right. Pivot index=2. Left subarray has 2 elements, right has 2, balanced split.

testCases.expected: balanced or unbalanced based on random choice

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Learn how to swap two elements in an array, a fundamental operation in partitioning.
ladders.explanation: Swapping is a basic operation where two elements in an array exchange positions. It is essential in algorithms like partition to rearrange elements around a pivot. The intuition is simple: to swap elements at indices i and j, we need a temporary variable to hold one value during the exchange to avoid overwriting. Steps: 1) Store the value at index i in a temp variable. 2) Assign the value at index j to index i. 3) Assign the temp value to index j. This ensures both values are correctly swapped. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant-time assignments. Space complexity is O(1) for the temp variable. Connection to L3: Swapping is used when selecting a random pivot or during the partition process. Common mistakes: forgetting to use a temp variable, leading to loss of data (e.g., if you do arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = arr[i], both end up with the same value). To avoid, always use a temp or, in languages like Python, use tuple assignment: arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]. Real code pattern: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. This operation is foundational for in-place algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: temp = arr[0] = 1. Step 2: arr[0] = arr[2] = 3. Step 3: arr[2] = temp = 1. Array is now [3, 2, 1].
Step 1: temp = arr[1] = 10. Step 2: arr[1] = arr[1] = 10. Step 3: arr[1] = temp = 10. No change as indices are same.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Indexing and Length
ladders.desc: Learn basic array concepts: indexing, length, and accessing elements.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element is accessed by an index, which is an integer representing its position. In most programming languages, indexing starts at 0, so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The length of an array is the number of elements it contains. For an array of length n, valid indices range from 0 to n-1. Accessing an element by index is an O(1) operation because it uses direct memory addressing. The intuition is that arrays provide fast random access, making them ideal for algorithms like QuickSelect that need to quickly compare and swap elements. To find the median, we need to compute indices based on length: for odd length, median index is n//2; for even, indices are n//2 - 1 and n//2. Connection to L4: Swapping requires knowledge of indices to specify which elements to exchange. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing by mistake, accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., index n), or misunderstanding integer division for median calculations. To avoid, remember that indices start at 0, and always check that indices are within 0 to n-1. Real code pattern: In Python, arr = [1, 2, 3]; first_element = arr[0]; length = len(arr); last_index = length - 1. This foundation is essential for all array manipulations.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has 3 elements. Index 0 accesses the first element: arr[0] = 10. Index 1: arr[1] = 20. Index 2: arr[2] = 30. Length n=3, so valid indices are 0,1,2. Last index is n-1 = 2.

examples.output: median indices for even length: 1 and 2
examples.trace: Step 1: n=4, even. Median is average of two middle elements. Compute indices: n//2 - 1 = 4//2 - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1; n//2 = 4//2 = 2. So indices 1 and 2: arr[1]=15, arr[2]=25. Median = (15+25)/2 = 20.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Two-Heap (Min-Max Heap) Method
approaches.summary: Maintain two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half of the array elements. Balance the heaps to efficiently compute the median by accessing the top elements.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for building, O(log n) for updates

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Median Using Two-Heap Method
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of median finding using min-max heap balancing approach.
ladders.explanation: The two-heap method provides an efficient way to find the median by maintaining two balanced heaps that represent the lower and upper halves of the data. The intuition is that by keeping a max-heap for the lower half (containing values less than or equal to the median) and a min-heap for the upper half (containing values greater than or equal to the median), we can access the median in constant time by looking at the top elements. This works because heaps allow us to efficiently maintain the sorted order property without fully sorting the entire array. The algorithm processes elements one by one, adding each to the appropriate heap based on its value relative to current heap tops, then rebalancing to ensure the heaps differ in size by at most 1. For odd total counts, the median is the top of the larger heap; for even counts, it's the average of both heap tops. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize empty max-heap (lower) and min-heap (upper). 2) For each element x: a) If max-heap empty or x ≤ max-heap top, add to max-heap else add to min-heap. b) Rebalance: if max-heap size > min-heap size + 1, move top from max to min. if min-heap size > max-heap size, move top from min to max. 3) After processing all elements: if total elements odd → return top of larger heap; if even → return average of both tops. Time complexity is O(n log n) for building (n insertions with O(log n) heap operations each) and O(n) space for storing elements. This connects to the full problem by providing the complete solution framework. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty heaps when peeking tops, incorrect rebalancing logic, and improper handling of negative numbers in max-heap implementations. Real code pattern uses heapq with negation for max-heap simulation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize max_heap=[], min_heap=[]
Step 2: Process 3 → max_heap empty → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-3], min_heap=[]
Step 3: Process 1 → 1 ≤ -(-3)=3 → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-3,-1], min_heap=[] → rebalance: max_heap size 2 > min_heap size 0 + 1 → move -3 to min_heap: max_heap=[-1], min_heap=[3]
Step 4: Process 4 → 4 > -(-1)=1 → add to min_heap: max_heap=[-1], min_heap=[3,4] → rebalance: min_heap size 2 > max_heap size 1 → move 3 to max_heap: max_heap=[-3,-1], min_heap=[4]
Step 5: Process 2 → 2 ≤ -(-3)=3 → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-3,-1,-2], min_heap=[4] → rebalance: max_heap size 3 > min_heap size 1 + 1 → move -3 to min_heap: max_heap=[-2,-1], min_heap=[3,4]
Step 6: Process 5 → 5 > -(-2)=2 → add to min_heap: max_heap=[-2,-1], min_heap=[3,4,5] → rebalance: min_heap size 3 > max_heap size 2 → move 3 to max_heap: max_heap=[-3,-2,-1], min_heap=[4,5]
Step 7: Total elements=5 (odd) → median = top of larger heap (max_heap) = -(-3)=3.0

Step 1: Initialize max_heap=[], min_heap=[]
Step 2: Process 7 → max_heap empty → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-7], min_heap=[]
Step 3: Process 3 → 3 ≤ -(-7)=7 → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-7,-3], min_heap=[] → rebalance: max_heap size 2 > min_heap size 0 + 1 → move -7 to min_heap: max_heap=[-3], min_heap=[7]
Step 4: Process 5 → 5 > -(-3)=3 → add to min_heap: max_heap=[-3], min_heap=[5,7] → rebalance: min_heap size 2 > max_heap size 1 → move 5 to max_heap: max_heap=[-5,-3], min_heap=[7]
Step 5: Process 2 → 2 ≤ -(-5)=5 → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-5,-3,-2], min_heap=[7] → rebalance: max_heap size 3 > min_heap size 1 + 1 → move -5 to min_heap: max_heap=[-3,-2], min_heap=[5,7]
Step 6: Total elements=4 (even) → median = (top of max_heap + top of min_heap)/2 = (-(-3)=3 + 5)/2 = 8/2 = 4.0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Heap Balancing After Insertion
ladders.desc: The rebalancing logic that maintains the two-heap structure after each insertion.
ladders.explanation: The heap balancing sub-routine is the core mechanism that ensures the two-heap structure correctly represents the sorted order of elements. The intuition is that after inserting any element into either heap, we must maintain the invariant that the max-heap contains the lower half of elements and the min-heap contains the upper half, with their sizes differing by at most 1. This balancing ensures that the median is always accessible from the heap tops. The algorithm works by checking size conditions: if the max-heap (lower half) has more than one extra element compared to the min-heap (upper half), we move the largest element from the max-heap to the min-heap; conversely, if the min-heap becomes larger than the max-heap, we move the smallest element from the min-heap to the max-heap. This redistribution maintains the property that all elements in max-heap are ≤ all elements in min-heap. Step-by-step: 1) After inserting element x into appropriate heap. 2) If size(max_heap) > size(min_heap) + 1: pop top from max_heap, push to min_heap. 3) If size(min_heap) > size(max_heap): pop top from min_heap, push to max_heap. Time complexity is O(log n) for the heap operations, and space is O(1) for temporary variables. This connects to L0 by being the critical maintenance operation called after each insertion in the full solution. Common mistakes include incorrect size comparison operators, forgetting to handle the case when both heaps are empty, and not properly transferring elements between heaps (including sign handling for max-heap implementations). Real code pattern uses heapq.heappush and heapq.heappop with negation for max-heap.

examples.output: max_heap=[-5,-4,-3,-1], min_heap=[7,9] → after balance: max_heap=[-4,-3,-1], min_heap=[5,7,9]
examples.trace:
Initial: max_heap size=3, min_heap size=2, max_heap tops: -5 (value 5), min_heap top: 7
Step 1: Insert 4 into max_heap (since 4 ≤ 5): max_heap=[-5,-4,-3,-1], min_heap=[7,9]
Step 2: Check balance: max_heap size=4, min_heap size=2 → max_heap size > min_heap size + 1? 4 > 2+1=3 → True
Step 3: Pop top from max_heap: pop -5 (value 5), push 5 to min_heap: max_heap=[-4,-3,-1], min_heap=[5,7,9]
Step 4: Re-check: max_heap size=3, min_heap size=3 → conditions satisfied

examples.output: max_heap=[-2], min_heap=[4,6,8] → after balance: max_heap=[-4,-2], min_heap=[6,8]
examples.trace:
Initial: max_heap size=1, min_heap size=2, max_heap top: -2 (value 2), min_heap top: 4
Step 1: Insert 8 into min_heap (since 8 > 2): max_heap=[-2], min_heap=[4,6,8]
Step 2: Check balance: max_heap size=1, min_heap size=3 → min_heap size > max_heap size? 3 > 1 → True
Step 3: Pop top from min_heap: pop 4, push -4 to max_heap: max_heap=[-4,-2], min_heap=[6,8]
Step 4: Re-check: max_heap size=2, min_heap size=2 → conditions satisfied

testCases.expected: max_heap=[-9,-5], min_heap=[8,12] → after: max_heap=[-8,-9,-5], min_heap=[12] → after: max_heap=[-8,-5], min_heap=[9,12]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Sorted Order with Two Heaps
ladders.desc: The fundamental technique of using max-heap for lower half and min-heap for upper half to track median.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the two-heap method is the insight that we can maintain a dynamically sorted view of data using two heaps without fully sorting the entire dataset. The intuition is that by splitting the data into two halves around the median, we can use a max-heap to efficiently track the largest element in the lower half (which would be at or just below the median) and a min-heap to track the smallest element in the upper half (which would be at or just above the median). This works because heaps provide O(1) access to their extreme values while allowing O(log n) insertions. The key invariant is: all elements in the max-heap (lower half) are ≤ all elements in the min-heap (upper half). When this invariant holds and the heaps are balanced (sizes differ by at most 1), the median is easily accessible: for odd total count, it's the top of the larger heap; for even, it's the average of both tops. The algorithm maintains this by always inserting new elements into the appropriate heap based on comparison with current heap tops, then rebalancing. This approach is efficient because it avoids the O(n log n) cost of repeated full sorts when processing streaming data. Time complexity per insertion is O(log n) for heap operations, and space is O(n) for storing all elements. This connects to L1 as the conceptual foundation for why the balancing subroutine works. Common mistakes include violating the invariant by incorrect insertion logic, not handling empty heaps during comparisons, and misunderstanding the heap property (max-heap stores largest at root, not smallest). Real code pattern maintains the invariant through careful comparison before insertion.

examples.output: After processing: max_heap=[-3,-1], min_heap=[4], median accessible from tops
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty heaps: max_heap=[], min_heap=[]
Step 2: Insert 3: max_heap empty → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-3], min_heap=[]
Invariant: max_heap contains lower half {3}, min_heap empty
Step 3: Insert 1: compare 1 with max_heap top -(-3)=3 → 1 ≤ 3 → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-3,-1], min_heap=[]
Rebalance: max_heap size 2 > min_heap size 0+1 → move -3 to min_heap: max_heap=[-1], min_heap=[3]
Invariant restored: all in max_heap {-1} ≤ all in min_heap {3}
Step 4: Insert 4: compare 4 with max_heap top -(-1)=1 → 4 > 1 → add to min_heap: max_heap=[-1], min_heap=[3,4]
Rebalance: min_heap size 2 > max_heap size 1 → move 3 to max_heap: max_heap=[-3,-1], min_heap=[4]
Final invariant: max_heap {-3,-1} ≤ min_heap {4}, sizes balanced (2 vs 1)

Step 1: Start: max_heap=[], min_heap=[]
Step 2: Insert 5: max_heap empty → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-5], min_heap=[]
Step 3: Insert 2: 2 ≤ 5 → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-5,-2], min_heap=[] → rebalance: move -5 to min_heap: max_heap=[-2], min_heap=[5]
Invariant: max_heap {2} ≤ min_heap {5}
Step 4: Insert 7: 7 > 2 → add to min_heap: max_heap=[-2], min_heap=[5,7] → rebalance: move 5 to max_heap: max_heap=[-5,-2], min_heap=[7]
Invariant: max_heap {5,2} ≤ min_heap {7}
Step 5: Insert 1: 1 ≤ 5 → add to max_heap: max_heap=[-5,-2,-1], min_heap=[7] → rebalance: move -5 to min_heap: max_heap=[-2,-1], min_heap=[5,7]
Final: max_heap {2,1} ≤ min_heap {5,7}, sizes equal (2 vs 2), median = (2+5)/2

testCases.expected: max_heap=[-2,-1], min_heap=[] → after balance: max_heap=[-1], min_heap=[2]
max_heap=[-4,-2], min_heap=[6] → after balance: max_heap=[-2], min_heap=[4,6] → after: max_heap=[-4,-2], min_heap=[6]
max_heap=[-6,-4], min_heap=[8,2] → invariant violated, corrected to max_heap=[-4,-2], min_heap=[6,8]

ladders.title: Building Block — Heap Data Structure Operations
ladders.desc: Understanding and implementing basic heap operations: insert, peek, and extract-min/max.
ladders.explanation: Heaps are tree-based data structures that satisfy the heap property: in a min-heap, each parent node is ≤ its children (so the minimum is at the root); in a max-heap, each parent is ≥ its children (so the maximum is at the root). The intuition is that heaps provide efficient access to extreme values while maintaining relatively efficient insertion and deletion. This makes them ideal for problems requiring repeated access to minimum or maximum elements, like maintaining the median through two heaps. The key operations are: 1) Insert (push): Add element to the end, then 'bubble up' by swapping with parent until heap property is restored — O(log n) time. 2) Peek (top): Return root element without removal — O(1) time. 3) Extract-min/max (pop): Remove root, move last element to root, then 'bubble down' by swapping with smallest/largest child until heap property restored — O(log n) time. In Python, heapq module provides min-heap operations; for max-heap, we store negatives. Step-by-step insert for min-heap: a) Append element to array. b) Set current index = last position. c) While current > 0 and array[current] < array[parent]: swap, update current = parent. Extract-min: a) Store root value. b) Move last element to root. c) Set current = 0. d) While has children: find smallest child, if array[current] > array[child]: swap, update current = child. Time complexity is O(log n) for insert/extract, O(1) for peek, O(n) space. This connects to L2 as the fundamental data structure enabling the two-heap method. Common mistakes include incorrect index calculations for parent/children (parent = (i-1)//2, left = 2*i+1, right = 2*i+2), forgetting to handle empty heap cases, and misunderstanding that heapq in Python is min-heap only. Real code pattern uses heapq.heappush and heapq.heappop.

examples.trace:
Initial heap (array representation): [3, 7, 5]
Step 1: Insert 2: append to end → [3, 7, 5, 2]
Step 2: Bubble up: index 3, parent index = (3-1)//2 = 1, value 7 > 2? Yes → swap → [3, 2, 5, 7]
Step 3: Continue: index 1, parent index = (1-1)//2 = 0, value 3 > 2? Yes → swap → [2, 3, 5, 7]
Step 4: Index 0, stop. Final heap: [2, 3, 5, 7]

Initial max-heap (stored as negatives for min-heap): [-5, -3, -4] represents values [5, 3, 4]
Step 1: Insert -2: append → [-5, -3, -4, -2]
Step 2: Bubble up: index 3, parent index = 1, value -3 > -2? No (since -3 < -2 in min-heap) → swap → [-5, -2, -4, -3]
Step 3: Continue: index 1, parent index = 0, value -5 > -2? No → swap → [-2, -5, -4, -3]
Step 4: Index 0, stop. Final heap: [-2, -5, -4, -3] which represents max-heap values [2, 5, 4, 3]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Swapping Elements
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of comparing two values and swapping their positions in an array.
ladders.explanation: Comparing and swapping are the most basic operations in many algorithms, including heap operations. The intuition is that by comparing elements, we can determine their relative order, and by swapping, we can rearrange them into desired positions. Comparison involves evaluating whether one value is less than, greater than, or equal to another, typically using relational operators (<, >, <=, >=, ==). Swapping exchanges the positions of two elements in a data structure, usually by using a temporary variable to hold one value during the exchange. In heap operations, comparisons determine whether the heap property is violated (e.g., a child being smaller than its parent in a min-heap), and swaps correct these violations during bubble-up or bubble-down processes. Step-by-step swap in an array: 1) Store value at index i in temp variable. 2) Assign value at index j to index i. 3) Assign temp value to index j. For comparison, simply use if statements with relational operators. These operations have O(1) time complexity and use O(1) extra space. This connects to L3 as the atomic actions performed within heap insert and extract operations. Common mistakes include forgetting to use a temporary variable during swap (leading to data loss), incorrect comparison operators (e.g., using < instead of > for max-heap), and off-by-one errors in array indices. Real code pattern: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp; and if arr[i] < arr[j]: ...

examples.input: Array [5, 3, 8], compare index 0 and 1, swap if out of order for min-heap
examples.trace:
Initial array: [5, 3, 8]
Step 1: Compare elements at indices 0 and 1: arr[0]=5, arr[1]=3
Step 2: Check if 5 > 3 (for min-heap, parent should be ≤ child) → True, so need swap
Step 3: Swap: temp = arr[0] = 5; arr[0] = arr[1] = 3; arr[1] = temp = 5
Step 4: Result: [3, 5, 8]

examples.input: Array [10, 20, 15], compare index 1 and 2 for max-heap property
examples.trace:
Initial array: [10, 20, 15]
Step 1: Compare elements at indices 1 and 2: arr[1]=20, arr[2]=15
Step 2: Check max-heap property: parent (index 1) should be ≥ child (index 2) → 20 ≥ 15? True
Step 3: Since condition satisfied, no swap performed
Step 4: Array remains [10, 20, 15]

testCases.input: Array [7, 2], compare and swap for min-heap
Array [6, 3, 5], compare indices 0 and 2, swap if 6>5
Array [100, 50, 75], compare indices 1 and 2 for max-heap
Array [9, 6, 3], swap indices 0 and 2 without comparison

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Numbers and Ordering
ladders.desc: Basic concepts of numerical values and how to compare them using relational operators.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level covers the fundamental understanding of numbers and their ordering, which is essential for any comparison-based algorithm like the two-heap median method. Numbers are mathematical objects used for counting, measuring, and labeling; in programming, they are typically represented as integers or floating-point values. Ordering refers to arranging numbers from smallest to largest (ascending) or largest to smallest (descending). The intuition is that by comparing numbers, we can determine their relative positions in an ordered sequence. This is done using relational operators: less than (<), greater than (>), less than or equal to (<=), greater than or equal to (>=), and equal to (==). For example, 3 < 5 means 3 is less than 5, so in ascending order, 3 comes before 5. Negative numbers follow the same rules: -5 < -3 because -5 is further left on the number line. Step-by-step comparison: 1) Identify the two numbers. 2) Apply the appropriate operator based on desired order. 3) The result is a boolean (true/false) indicating whether the relationship holds. This has O(1) time complexity and no extra space. This connects to L4 as the prerequisite for performing comparison operations in heap algorithms. Common mistakes include confusing < and > operators, misunderstanding negative number ordering, and not considering floating-point precision issues. Real code pattern uses simple if statements like if a < b: or while x >= y:.

examples.input: Compare 7 and 3 using less than operator
examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify numbers: 7 and 3
Step 2: Apply operator: 7 < 3
Step 3: Evaluate: Is 7 less than 3? On number line, 7 is to the right of 3, so 7 is greater than 3
Step 4: Result: false

examples.input: Order the numbers -2, 0, 1, -5 in ascending order
examples.trace:
Step 1: List numbers: -2, 0, 1, -5
Step 2: Recall number line order: ... -5, -4, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...
Step 3: Compare: -5 is smallest (leftmost), then -2, then 0, then 1
Step 4: Ascending order (smallest to largest): -5, -2, 0, 1
